title: Sinusoids
summary: This page presents Example B.6, demonstrating how to express the sum of two sinusoids as a single sinusoid using phasor addition and Eq. (B.17). Two cases are worked out: (a) cos(ω0t) − √3 sin(ω0t) = 2cos(ω0t + 60°) and (b) −3cos(ω0t) + 4sin(ω0t) with C=5 and θ=−126.9°. Phasor diagrams (Fig. B.8) are used to verify the results geometrically.

figures.caption: Phasor diagrams illustrating (a) the sum of two sinusoids represented as a phasor of length 2 at 60°, and (b) a phasor of length 5 at −126.9° corresponding to the combination of real part −3 and imaginary part −4.
figures.description: Two phasor diagrams are shown on complex-plane axes labeled Re (horizontal) and Im (vertical). In (a), a vector from the origin has magnitude 2 at an angle of 60°, with dashed projections marking real part 1 and imaginary part √3; students should notice how rectangular components map to a polar phasor. In (b), a vector of magnitude 5 points into the third quadrant at −126.9°, with dashed lines indicating real part −3 and imaginary part −4; students should notice the correspondence between negative Cartesian components and the phasor’s length and angle.
